summary: Jeff Atwood explores how JavaScript, HTML, and CSS are designed to be extremely forgiving of errors, hiding mistakes rather than halting execution. After accidentally leaving script debugging enabled, he discovered the web is riddled with JavaScript errors that browsers deliberately suppress. He contrasts this permissive approach with strict languages that punish programmers for tiny mistakes, and examines how XHTML's draconian error handling doomed it to failure. Atwood concludes that forgiveness by default, while alien to programmers conditioned to appreciate strict compilers, is essential for the web's massive global adoption.
key_points: The web is saturated with JavaScript errors that browsers deliberately hide from users to maintain usability
HTML and CSS exhibit bizarrely tolerant behavior, producing weird but functional results from invalid input rather than errors
XHTML's strict error handling - rendering nothing and showing error messages for any malformed markup - caused it to fail as a standard
Programmers trained on strict compilers have been conditioned to appreciate punishment for mistakes, but this approach doesn't scale for the web
Forgiveness by default enables the large-scale, worldwide adoption that made the web successful
money_quotes: The web is full of JavaScript errors. You can barely click through three links before encountering a JavaScript error of one kind or another.
Things that would cause show stopping compiler errors in any other language are at worst minor inconveniences in JavaScript.
When errors are ignored by default, what you end up with is an incredibly tolerant, extremely permissive programming ecosystem. If it works, it works, errors be damned.
I can't think of any other programming environment that goes to such lengths to avoid presenting error messages, that tries so hard to make broken code work, at least a little.
The permissive, flexible tolerance designed into HTML and JavaScript is alien to programmers who grew up being regularly flagellated by their compiler for the tiniest of mistakes.
Some of us were punished so much so that we actually started to like it.
They may not have realized it at the time, but the Draconians inadvertently destroyed the future of XHTML with this single, irrevocable decision.
Even though programmers have learned to like draconian strictness, forgiveness by default is what works.
key_insight: The web's success depends on its forgiving nature - strict error handling that programmers are trained to appreciate would have prevented the internet's global adoption.
